coredata.dc:title: Fluorine-doped carbon dots decorated on iron-cobalt selenide nanosheets as superior electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution in seawater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Jiaxu
Zhang, Longyue
Zhang, Zheng
Chen, Hao
Hu, Yaoping
Li, Guochang
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Developing oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalysts that can efficiently and stably operate at high current densities in seawater electrolysis is urgently demanded but challenging. In this study, selenium-vacancies-rich iron-cobalt selenide nanosheets (FeCoSe-VSe) decorated with fluorine-doped carbon dots (FCDs) are rationally designed and prepared on nickel foams (NF) through the successive processes of hydrothermal treatment and selenization. Benefiting from the large accessible surface area and abundant active sites provided by FCDs and selenium vacancy defects, as well as the coupled interface between FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe that optimizes the oxygen adsorption energy and accelerates the kinetic process, the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF composite exhibits excellent OER catalytic activities with a low overpotential of 258 mV at a current density of 200 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH and a small Tafel slope of 34.7 mV dec–1. In the electrolytes of simulated and natural seawater, the overpotentials of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrode are slightly higher, measured to be 264 and 297 mV, respectively. More profoundly, the introduction of FCDs enhances the corrosion resistance of the catalyst in alkaline electrolytes, ensuring the long-term stability. This work offers a guidance to improve the electrochemical performances of catalysts for high-efficiency seawater oxidation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorine-doped carbon dots
Iron-cobalt selenide nanosheets
Selenium vacancies
Oxygen evolution reaction
Seawater

C. Wong Magna Fund at Ningbo University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration for the preparation of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF.

(a,b) SEM images of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF and FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF, (c,d) TEM and HRTEM images, (e) HAADF-STEM image and the corresponding elemental mappings of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe nanosheets. Inset in (c): HRTEM image of a single FCD.
(a) XRD pattern of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF, (b) XPS Fe 2p, and (c) Co 2p spectra of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF and FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF, (d) Se 3d, (e) C 1 s, and (f) F 1 s spectra of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF.

(a) LSV curves, (b) corresponding Tafel plots, (c) Nyquist plots, (d) Cdl values, and (e) LSV curves standardized by ECSA of different electrocatalysts, (f) OER stability test of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst. Inset in (f): the LSV curves of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst before and after continuous test for 200 h.
(a) LSV curves, (b) Tafel plots, (c) Nyquist plots, and (d) OER stability test of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF in different electrolytes.
Comparison of the OER performance of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF with other electrocatalysts in 1 M KOH.

head.ce:title: Fluorine-doped carbon dots decorated on iron-cobalt selenide nanosheets as superior electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution in seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalysts that can efficiently and stably operate at high current densities in seawater electrolysis is urgently demanded but challenging. In this study, selenium-vacancies-rich iron-cobalt selenide nanosheets (FeCoSe-VSe) decorated with fluorine-doped carbon dots (FCDs) are rationally designed and prepared on nickel foams (NF) through the successive processes of hydrothermal treatment and selenization. Benefiting from the large accessible surface area and abundant active sites provided by FCDs and selenium vacancy defects, as well as the coupled interface between FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe that optimizes the oxygen adsorption energy and accelerates the kinetic process, the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF composite exhibits excellent OER catalytic activities with a low overpotential of 258 mV at a current density of 200 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH and a small Tafel slope of 34.7 mV dec–1. In the electrolytes of simulated and natural seawater, the overpotentials of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrode are slightly higher, measured to be 264 and 297 mV, respectively. More profoundly, the introduction of FCDs enhances the corrosion resistance of the catalyst in alkaline electrolytes, ensuring the long-term stability. This work offers a guidance to improve the electrochemical performances of catalysts for high-efficiency seawater oxidation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With drastic consumption of non-renewable resources and serious threat of ever deteriorating environment, there is an urgent need to find a clean, efficient, and low cost technology for energy storage and conversion [1,2]. Water electrolysis technology, characterized by high product purity, environmental friendliness, and renewability, is a promising way to solve the problem [3]. The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) (4OH– → O2 + 4e– + 2H2O) at the anode and the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) (2H2O + 2e– → H2 + 2OH–) at the cathode are the two reactions for water decomposition [4,5]. Among them, the OER process involves four-electron transfer, leading to kinetic retardation and energy loss, and requires a considerable overpotential to drive the electrochemical reaction, which severely hampers the efficiency of water electrolysis [6,7]. Most of the studies about OER in electrolysis use freshwater as the electrolyte. Compared to freshwater, seawater is of great significance for coastal areas as it can be used for desalination and conserve scarce freshwater [8]. However, the presence of Cl– in seawater not only has the potential to generate ClO– (generated at overpotentials > 480 mV), but also corrodes the electrocatalyst, thus limiting its catalytic performance and practical applications [9]. In order to improve the energy conversion performance in seawater electrolysis, exploring an efficient and long-term stable OER electrocatalyst is necessary.

To date, iridium/ruthenium-based materials are considered to be excellent catalysts for OER [10]. However, the scarcity and high cost of precious metals limit their widespread use [11]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a cheap, readily available, and robust electrocatalyst to replace precious metals for further development of water electrolysis technology [12]. Against this background, scientists have turned their attention to transition metals such as iron, cobalt, nickel, manganese, and molybdenum, and find that transition metal compounds, especially transition metal selenides (TMSs), are good candidates as OER catalysts [13–15], because i) TMSs have prominent d-electron configuration and high electrical conductivity [16]; ii) selenium has a large radius, low ionization energy, and low electronegativity, which gives TMSs the ability to transfer charges quickly [17,18]; iii) the introduction of vacancy defects in TMSs can increase active sites and reconstruct electronic states to improve the surface activity of the electrocatalysts [19]. Nowadays, great progress has been achieved in engineering the nanostructures of TMSs. However, the rational design of TMS-based OER catalysts with enhanced catalytic capability and stability is still a challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterials, have been widely used in catalyst design due to their low price, good electrical conductivity, and abundant functional groups [20]. CDs can crosslink with metal ions to form 3D structures for application as OER catalysts, which not only avoid the aggregation of metal substances, but also ensure the stability of the materials [21]. Additionally, doping heteroatoms like N, P, and F in CDs can increase the active sites and promote electron transfer [22]. Many efforts have been made to construct CD-related electrode materials. For example, Lu group used halogen (X = F, Cl, and Br)-doped CDs to modify amorphous CoP for preparation of the XCDs/CoP composites; among which, FCDs/CoP processed an outstanding OER activity due to the optimized oxygen adsorption energy and accelerated water splitting kinetics induced by coupling interface between FCDs and CoP [21]. Despite some impressive advances, research on facile synthesis of CDs-related nanomaterials as advanced OER electrocatalysts is still in an early stage.

In this work, iron-cobalt layered double hydroxide (FeCo-LDH) decorated with fluorine-doped carbon dots (FCDs) are synthesized on nickel foams (NF) by a hydrothermal step, and then, selenization method is used to treat with the above FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF composite in the atmosphere of Ar/H2 to prepare FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF with rich selenium vacancies (Fig. 1 ). The obtained FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF exhibits excellent OER performances, with a low overpotential of 258 mV at a current density of 200 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH and a small Tafel slope of 34.7 mV dec–1. Importantly, the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF catalyst possesses good stability with negligible degradation of catalytic performance after 200 h of continuous testing. Moreover, using simulated and natural seawater as the electrolytes, the overpotentials of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrode do not remarkably increase, achieving the satisfactory values of 264 and 297 mV, respectively. The outstanding OER performance of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF is attributed to the large specific surface area and abundant active sites provided by FCDs and selenium vacancy defects, the coupled interface between FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe, as well as the synergistic effect of bimetallic Fe-Co componentsin the composite.

Selenium powder was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate was obtained from Shanghai Boer Chemical Regents Co., Ltd. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, ammonium fluoride, urea, potassium hydroxide, sodium chloride, hydrochloric acid, and ethanol were supplied by Macklin Chemical Co., Ltd.

The FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF composite was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. To ensure the cleanliness of the nickel foam (NF) surface, it was ultrasonically cleaned with hydrochloric acid, deionized water and ethanol before use. Firstly, 2 mmol of cobalt nitrate, 1 mmol of iron nitrate, 5 mmol of urea and 2.5 mmol of ammonium fluoride were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon sealed autoclave with a piece of pretreated NF (2 cm × 3 cm) and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After reaction, the product was washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried to obtain FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF. For comparison, different molar ratios of Fe/Co metal sources (3:0, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 0:3, with a total molar mass of 3 mmol) were added without changing other synthetic conditions. Notice that linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) tests were performed directly on different ratios of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF precursors, and the catalyst with the optimal OER activity was selected for subsequent selenization experiments and characterization tests, which are no longer purposely labeled in the naming of the samples. For synthesis of FeCo-LDH/NF without modification of FCDs, 5 mmol of urea (serving as the carbon source for FCDs) was replaced by 7 mmol of NaOH and 2.25 mmol of Na2CO3, and other synthetic procedures were the same as those of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF.

The FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF composite was prepared by the H2 reduction strategy. A porcelain boat containing Se powder (0.5 g) was placed upstream of the quartz tube, and the above obtained FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF was placed downstream. FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF was then annealed at 350 °C for 2 h in Ar/H2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 to obtain the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF composite. Temperature- and time-controlled samples were prepared as described in the Supplementary Material.

A noble metal based RuO2 electrode was prepared for comparison of the OER performance. Firstly, 40 mg of RuO2 and 60 μL of Nafion solution were dispersed in 540 μL of ethanol and 400 μL of deionized water in a small sealed tube. Subsequently, the mixture was sonicated for 30 min before the dispersion was coated on a NF substrate and dried in air overnight.

Morphology and microstructures of the materials were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Nova NanoSEM 450) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200x). Crystal phases of the samples were identified by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) with Cu Ka radiation. Raman spectra were measured using a 532 nm laser on LabRam HR Evolution. Elemental compositions and valence states of the materials were analyzed by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi + ) with Al Ka radiation. Vacancies were detected by paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR, Bruker EMXplus-6/1).

Fig. 1 illustrates a two-step synthetic strategy for preparation of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF composite through hydrothermal treatment followed by selenization in the Ar/H2 atmosphere. In the first step, urea not only acts as the carbon source for conversion to CDs, but also binds with metal ions to generate hydroxides during hydrothermal decomposition. NH4F has two functions: i) it provides the heteroatoms of fluorine for doping in CDs; ii) it serves as a structural guide to synthesize the material with large surface area. In the second step, FeCo-LDH in the FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF composite is selenized by the selenium vapor, forming FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF with rich selenium vacancies. As shown in Fig. S1, the color of NF changes from silver-white to orange after hydrothermal reaction, and further turns to silver-black after selenization, indicating the successful preparation of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst. It is worth mentioning that NF provides the interconnected porous structure, which can facilitate electron conduction and water transfer, and thus is helpful to enhance the OER performance of the composite.

The morphologies and structures of the materials are first investigated. In the SEM image of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF (Fig. 2 a), smooth-surfaced nanosheets made of FeCo-LDH are densely and uniformly grown on NF. After selenization, the produced FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF retains the morphological feature of the precursor, but the surface of FeCoSe-VSe becomes rough and curled (Fig. 2b), demonstrating the increased specific surface area. The ultrathin nanosheet structure and the high-density voids generated by interconnections in FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF can expose more catalytic sites to the electrolyte, provide fast transport channels for electrons/ions, and promote O2 desorption, which are beneficial to improve the OER catalytic performance. The FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe nanosheets are isolated from the NF framework for further inspection by TEM. Fig. 2c shows that a lot of nanosized dots with diameters of 2–4 nm are well distributed on the FeCoSe-VSe nanosheets. In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 2c), the single dot possesses clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [23]. These observations prove that urea undergoes cross-linking carbonization during the hydrothermal process, generating FCDs. Fig. 2d displays different crystalline phases of CoFe2Se4, Co0.85Se, and FeSe2 in the nanosheets, and meanwhile, partial deletions of the lattice fringes are found, confirming the presence of selenium vacancies (VSe). FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe constitute a polycrystalline nanosheet composite containing many dislocations, grain boundary defects and vacancies, which are favorable for OER dynamics. The high-angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image and elemental mappings (Fig. 2e) reveal that besides Fe, Co, and Se, a certain amount of C, a small amount of F, as well as a tiny amount of N are uniformly distributed on the nanosheet, further proving the successful decoration of FCDs on FeCoSe-VSe.

XRD measurements are used to study the crystal structures of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF and FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF (Fig. S2 and Fig. 3 a). It should be noted that i) the peaks located near 44.5°, 51.8°, and 76.0° in the XRD patterns are ascribed to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of NF (Standard Card PDF#01–070-1849), respectively [24]; ii) due to the low content of FCDs contained in FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF and FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF, the XRD signal of FCDs is rather weak and hardly to be observed. Fig. S2 shows the diffraction peaks at 11.6°, 23.3°, and 38.7°, coinciding with the standard card of FeCo-LDH (PDF#50–0235) [25]. After selenization, the diffraction peaks of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF at 33.2°, 44.5°, and 50.3° in Fig. 3a correspond to the Co0.85Se phase (PDF#52–1008), while other diffraction peaks belong to CoFe2Se4 (PDF#52–2337) and FeSe2 (PDF#12–0291) or CoSe2 (the characteristic peaks of CoSe2 are similar to those of FeSe2). It is noteworthy that all the diffraction peaks of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF are slightly negatively shifted when compared to the standard cards, which may be induced by the selenium vacancies [26]. The presence of selenium vacancies is further confirmed by EPR testing. As shown in Fig. S3, the EPR spectrum of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF shows a pair of signal peaks at g = 2.005 from unpaired electrons trapped by anionic selenium vacancies. The intensity of the peak represents the amount of vacancy concentration. Obviously, the peak intensities of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst prepared in the Ar/H2 atmosphere are significantly stronger than those of FCDs/FeCoSe/NF prepared in the Ar atmosphere, indicating the successful introduction of Se vacancies [27]. The presence of vacancies can modulate the electronic structure of metal compound atoms as well as the energy barriers of intermediates and reactants to effectively enhance charge transfer and improve catalyst surface activity, thus enhancing OER performance.

The elemental states and chemical bonding of the composites are analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. S4, the XPS survey spectrum of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe nanosheets reveals that the material mainly consists of Fe, Co, Se, and C, and small quantities of F and N are also detected. It should be noted that the signal of O probably comes from the oxygenic functional groups of FCDs. In the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 711.9 and 725.2 eV correspond to the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 features, respectively, while the remaining two peaks are the satellites [28,29]. Compared with FCDs/FeCo-LDH, an additional peak belonging to the Fe–Se bond at 707.1 eV appears in FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe, which indicates that selenium has reacted with iron [25]. In the Co 2p spectrum of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe (Fig. 3c), the peaks at 797.3 eV and 783.6 eV are associated with Co2+ species, while the peaks at 794.3 eV and 780.6 eV accompanied by two satellite peaks are related to Co3+ species [30]. The diversity of Co valence states can accelerate electron transfer and promote redox reactions on the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe surface [31]. In comparison to FCDs/FeCo-LDH, an extra peak of Co–Se bond at 778.3 eV is observed in FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe. It should be pointed out that both Fe 2p3/2 and Co 2p3/2 in FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe undergo a slight positive shift compared to FCDs/FeCo-LDH, which may be caused by the oxidation of the metals after introduction of selenium vacancies [31]. The Se 3d spectrum (Fig. 3d) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 55.0 and 54.2 eV, which are assigned to Se 3d3/2 and Se 3d5/2 of metal–selenium bonds (SeM, MCo and Fe), respectively [32]. In addition, due to the formation of selenium vacancies and the likely exposure of the catalyst to air, the surface of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe is oxidized to form SeO related bonds, resulting in the emergency of another peak at 59.5 eV [17]. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3e), the fitted peaks at 284.7, 286.1, 288.1, and 289.3 eV are attributed to CC/CC, CO, CO, and CF bonds, respectively [33]. The F 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows the single peak of CF at 687.0 eV, indicating that F atoms have incorporated in CDs [34]. All the above data demonstrates the successful decoration of FCDs on FeCoSe-VSe, which offers more active sites for OER and improves the surface area of the electrocatalyst.

In order to obtain the electrocatalyst with optimal OER performance, the FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF precursors are first prepared by adding metal sources with different molar ratios (nCo: nFe = 3:0, 2:1, 1.5:1.5, 1:2, and 0:3), and their OER catalytic activities are evaluated by a typical three-electrode system in 1 M KOH electrolyte. From the LSV curves (Fig. S5), it is found that when nCo: nFe = 2:1, the precursor sample exhibits the best OER activity with an overpotential of 362 mV at a current density of 200 mA cm−2. The above-prepared FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF is then selenized at different temperatures for different times. Fig. S6 reveals that appropriately increasing the annealing temperature and time can result in a more complete selenization and thus enhance the OER catalytic activity. However, excessively high temperature makes the NF substrate brittle, which is detrimental to the OER activity and reduces the stability of the electrocatalyst. Too long annealing time leads to a large amount of selenium remaining on the surface of the electrocatalyst, thus blocking the active sites. Therefore, the optimal reaction temperature and time are determined to be 350 °C and 2 h, respectively. In such a synthetic condition, the obtained FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF shows the best OER activity, with a low overpotential of 258 mV at a current density of 200 mA cm−2.

Subsequently, the OER catalytic performances of the optimized FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst are systematically compared with other materials, including FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF, FCDs/FeCoSe/NF (synthesized in Ar atmosphere without H2), FeCoSe-VSe/NF (synthesized without FCDs raw materials), and the commercial RuO2 loaded on NF (RuO2/NF). It can be seen from the LSV curves (Fig. 4 a) that the OER activity of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst is significantly higher than that of other comparison samples, and a current density of 200 mA cm−2 can be provided at an overpotential of 258 mV, which is lower than that of FCDs/FeCo-LDH/NF and FCDs/FeCoSe/NF (286 mV), indicating that the introduction of selenium vacancies changes the electronic configuration, increases the catalytic active sites, and facilitates the transport of ions, thus enhancing the OER catalytic activity. It is also found that in comparison to FeCoSe-VSe/NF without decoration of FCDs, the OER performance of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF possesses a considerable enhancement due to the coupling interface between FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe that optimizes the oxygen adsorption energy and accelerates the kinetics of water splitting [21]. Impressively, the OER performance of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF is improved by 23.2 % over the benchmark RuO2/NF, revealing the great potential of the developed electrocatalyst for real-world applications. The Tafel slope reflects the kinetic characteristic of the electrocatalyst. In Fig. 4b, FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF has the smallest Tafel slope (34.7 mV dec–1), suggesting the highest transfer coefficients and strongest electrocatalytic kinetics among five different materials. The OER catalytic activity of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF is superior than most of other reported non-precious metal electrocatalysts [15,22,35–42], as shown in Table 1 . EIS tests are performed in the frequency range of 102–105 Hz. The low-frequency region exhibits a semicircle whose diameter represents the charge-transfer resistance, and a larger diameter denotes a higher charge-transfer resistance [43]. The EIS tests in Fig. 4c validate that the fastest charge transfer is achieved using the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of each catalyst is proportional to its double layer capacitance (Cdl), which can be used to assess the intrinsic surface area activity of the catalysts [44]. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the electrocatalysts (Fig. S7) are collected by selecting non-Faraday intervals and plotted in Fig. 4d. It is obvious that FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF has the largest Cdl value (86.5 mF cm−2), indicating that it has the largest ECSA, i.e., the highest density of exposed active sites. The ECSA normalized polarization curves in Fig. 4e further suggest that FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF has the highest intrinsic OER activity compared with other samples. The electrochemical stability of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF catalyst is examined by performing long-term stability tests at constant current density. As shown in Fig. 4f, the real-time potentials of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF remain unchanged with almost negligible fluctuations after 200 h continuous operation at constant current densities of 200 and 500 mA cm−2. The inset of Fig. 4f illustrates that the LSV curves of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF before and after 200 h continuous use are nearly completely overlapped, further testifying the good stability. The morphology of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF after stability test is observed by SEM (Fig. S8), in which it can be seen that the physical structure of the electrocatalyst does not change and no collapse of the framework occurs. It is deduced that the decoration of FCDs on the FeCoSe-VSe nanosheets is helpful to prevent the catalyst from clumping and being corroded in alkaline electrolytes, thus ensuring the long-term stability.

Seawater is one of the richest natural resources on earth. Using seawater rather than freshwater as the electrolyte for OER is more practical [45]. Therefore, the OER activities of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF electrocatalyst are also evaluated in alkaline simulated seawater (1 M KOH + 0.5 M NaCl) and alkaline natural seawater (1 M KOH + seawater). As shown in Fig. 5 a, the OER activity of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF catalyst in simulated seawater remains high, maintaining an overpotential of 264 mV at a current density of 200 mA cm−2, which is very close to the performance in 1 M KOH freshwater electrolyte. However, the OER activity of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF in alkaline natural seawater decreases in a certain extent, exhibiting a larger overpotential of 297 mV. The presence of various contaminants in natural seawater such as metal cations (Ca2+ and Mg2+), chloride ions (Cl–), and insoluble precipitates (including bacteria, microorganisms, and small particles), may damage the electrodes and bury some active sites of the electrocatalyst, thus resulting in the attenuation of OER performance. Even so, the overpotential of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF at a high current density of 200 mA cm−2 in natural seawater is still remarkably smaller than the overpotential required to initiate hypochlorite generation (480 mV). The Tafel slopes (Fig. 5b) and Nyquist plots (Fig. 5c) verify the decline of catalytic kinetics of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF in simulated and natural seawater. Fortunately, the OER performances of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF do not drop off in simulated and natural seawater after 200 h continuous use (Fig. 5d). All the Faraday efficiencies of O2 tested in the 1 M KOH, simulated and natural seawater are close to 100 % (Fig. S9), demonstrating that nearly all the transmitted electrons on the surface of electrode are involved in the production of O2. Fig. S10 shows that all the XPS C1s spectra of FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF before use and after 200 h use in 1 M KOH, simulated and natural seawater are quite similar, without significant difference, and the atomic concentrations of carbon in all four samples are almost the same, maintaining the value of about 30 %, indicating that FCDs do not decompose during the OER process. The above results prove the great stability of the electrocatalyst, which is crucial for practical applications in the energy field.

The outstanding OER performance of the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF catalyst is ascribed to the following aspects: i) the catalyst has a large specific surface area and an abundance of vacancy defects, which contributes to the efficient diffusion of the electrolyte and the rapid release of oxygen bubbles; ii) the self-supporting nanosheet structure ensures a firm adhesion between the catalyst and the substrate, which not only reduces the contact resistance for rapid charge transfer, but also improves the mechanical stability of the material and the durability of the electrocatalyst; iii) numerous FCDs decorated on the FeCoSe-VSe nanosheets provide richer active sites, and the coupled interface between FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe optimizes the oxygen adsorption energy and accelerates the kinetic process.

In summary, the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF composite with a unique 3D nanosheet array structure is designed and constructed by a two-step synthetic route of hydrothermal treatment and selenization. Thanks to the large accessible surface area and abundant active sites provided by FCDs and selenium vacancy defects, as well as the coupled interface between FCDs and FeCoSe-VSe that optimizes the oxygen adsorption energy and accelerates the kinetic process, the FCDs/FeCoSe-VSe/NF catalyst exhibits excellent OER catalytic activity and good durability in electrolytes of alkaline freshwater, simulated seawater, and natural seawater. This study provides a feasible strategy for rational design of electrocatalyst with outstanding OER performance in seawater electrolysis.

Jiaxu Dai: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Longyue Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zheng Zhang: Software, Methodology. Hao Chen: Visualization, Software. Yaoping Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Guochang Li: Supervision, Methodology. Lei Han: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the General Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Zhejiang Province (Y202351157), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund at Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.161456.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
